Other notable drill rappers include Lil Durk, King Von, Polo G, and G Herbo.

what is pluggnb

"Pluggnb" is a subgenre of hip hop that originated in the mid-2010s in Atlanta, Georgia. The term "plugg" refers to the producer or the "plug" who provides the beats or instrumentals, while "nb" stands for "new wave," indicating the new style or movement of the subgenre. Pluggnb is characterized by its heavy use of 808 drums, trap-style melodies, and slow, ominous beats. The subgenre gained popularity through the rise of artists such as Lil Baby, Gunna, and Lil Keed, who often work with pluggnb producers such as Turbo, Wheezy, and Tay Keith.
which producer is known for using sub bass

There are many producers that are known to incorporate sub bass into their beats, as it is a common element in many subgenres of hip-hop and electronic music. However, one of the most popular producers known for using sub bass in their music is Metro Boomin. Metro Boomin is a Grammy-nominated producer who has worked with numerous hip-hop artists, including Future, Drake, Lil Uzi Vert, and Post Malone, among others. He is known for his hard-hitting, bass-heavy beats, which often incorporate 808 drums and deep sub bass. Some of his most popular songs that feature sub bass include "Mask Off" by Future, "Congratulations" by Post Malone, and "Bad and Boujee" by Migos.
why is the 808 so popular in trap music

The 808 bass is popular in trap music because it provides a powerful, deep bass sound that contrasts with the fast-paced hi-hats and snares commonly used in the genre. The 808 drum machine was first introduced by Roland in the early 1980s, and was initially used in genres such as electro, hip hop, and dance music.

However, in the early 2010s, producers began to experiment with the 808 sound, using it to create new and unique basslines that would become synonymous with the trap genre. The 808 bass provides a heavy low-end punch that adds a sense of urgency and intensity to the music. It has become a staple sound in trap music, and is often combined with other elements such as aggressive synth sounds, distorted vocals, and complex hi-hat patterns to create the genre's signature sound.
who is considered to be the influencer of modern 808s in rap music

The origin of the 808 bass sound in rap music is often credited to the pioneering work of several producers such as Rick Rubin, 808 Mafia, Mannie Fresh, and DJ Toomp, among others. However, the producer who is often credited with popularizing the modern use of the 808 bass in rap music is Atlanta-based producer, Lex Luger.

Luger's production work in the early 2010s helped to define the sound of modern trap music and made heavy use of the 808 bass. His groundbreaking work on songs like Waka Flocka Flame's "Hard in Da Paint" and Rick Ross's "B.M.F." created a new standard for the use of 808s in rap music that many producers continue to follow today. Luger's style of using the 808 bass in combination with rapid hi-hats, snappy snares, and heavy synth sounds has since become a defining feature of modern rap production, and his influence can be heard in the work of many contemporary producers.
i thought it was ye

Kanye West is certainly a significant figure in hip-hop production, and he has used the 808 bass sound in many of his songs. His 2008 album "808s & Heartbreak" was a significant influence on the use of the 808 bass sound in modern rap music, including the heavy use of auto-tuned vocals that have since become a defining characteristic of trap music.

While Kanye West's use of the 808 bass sound was undoubtedly influential, it is generally believed that he was building on the work of preceding producers like Lex Luger and the other producers I mentioned earlier. Kanye West's contributions to hip-hop production are certainly significant, but when it comes to the use of the 808 bass in rap music, Lex Luger is the producer who is most often credited with popularizing its modern use.
how did 808s and heartbreak influence rap

808s & Heartbreak, despite its initial mixed reception from critics and fans, had a significant influence on the sound of modern rap music. The album was groundbreaking in the sense that it saw Kanye West abandon his traditional rap approach in favor of a more experimental and melodic sound, which would go on to become a defining characteristic of modern trap music.

One of the most significant ways that 808s & Heartbreak influenced rap was through its heavy use of the Roland TR-808 drum machine, which had a profound impact on the low-end rumble and bass drops that have since become synonymous with modern trap music. The album's use of autotuned vocals, melancholic melodies, and emotionally charged lyrics have also been widely emulated by modern hip-hop artists like Travis Scott, Lil Uzi Vert, and Juice WRLD, among others.

808s & Heartbreak's impact on rap music was not only musical but also cultural. The album is widely regarded as a pivotal moment in hip-hop when artists began to explore new styles and sounds, moving beyond the traditional boundaries of rap music. The record's emotional vulnerability and honest introspection also helped to pave the way for a new era of emotionally expressive rap music, which has become a hallmark of many contemporary hip-hop artists' styles.
when did the album release
Kanye West's album "808s & Heartbreak" was released on November 24, 2008.
